title: Capital budgeting, uncertainty, and misallocation
abstract: In canonical models of investment dynamics under uncertainty, вҖңtime-to-buildвҖқ in investment decisions implies that uncertainty negatively impacts firm values and aggregate capital productivity. However, capital budgeting, which involves ex-ante information acquisition and state-contingent investment decisions, can potentially ameliorate time-to-build frictions. Reduced-form evidence using firm-level data on sales and investment expectations and errors supports both mechanisms. Incorporating capital budgeting into a standard investment model, our calibrated model reveals that state-contingent investment planning and information acquisition reduce aggregate productivity losses by 41% and 17%, respectively. Moreover, gains from planning accrue primarily to less productive firms, while information acquisition benefits higher productivity ones.

author.Alan Kwan: The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR
author.Eugene Tan*: Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto, Canada

article.1. Introduction: A standard assumption of workhorse models of investment dynamics in macroeconomics and finance is that investment decisions are made ahead of time and are difficult to change even after new information is received. This assumption, commonly referred to as вҖңtime-to-buildвҖқ (Kydland and Prescott (1982)), means that incorrect forecasts today of future fundamentals can lead to under- or over-investment, reducing firm values and aggregate productivity (e.g., David et al. (2016); Bloom et al. (2018); Tanaka et al. (2020)).However, capital budgeting, which determines investment decisions, is more flexible in practice. While capital budgeting cannot undo the technological constraint of time-to-build, investment plans can often be structured to vary to some extent in response to new information. For instance, in 2020, two months after the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic, Emirates Airlines sought to cancel five of its original Airbus A380 orders. While doing so would incur a penalty of US$350 million, canceling their orders would avoid over-investment when demand for large long-haul flights is low.1 Although it may be costly, such investment flexibility partially insures the firm against uncertainty and reduces the impact of uncertainty on firm values and aggregate productivity.Despite its practical importance, to our knowledge, no model in macroeconomics or finance incorporates investment flexibility alongside information frictions when studying how uncertainty affects firm performance. Instead, recent literature has focused on information frictions in isolation, which overstates the role of information frictions if firms can adjust investment expenditures to a significant degree in response to real-time information. In this paper, we combine these elements into a model of investment under uncertainty. Together with a novel dataset, we quantify the impact of uncertainty and the value of investment flexibility in the capital budgeting process for firm performance and aggregate productivity.The contribution of our paper is two-fold. First, we provide new evidence on how firms' investment adjusts to new information about idiosyncratic productivity shocks. Our evidence suggests that while firms do acquire information to reduce uncertainty when making investment plans, actual investment also responds to new information after plans are made regarding profitability, indicating that firms rely on both mechanisms to mitigate the negative impact of uncertainty on firm value. Second, building upon our stylized facts, we develop a parsimonious model with endogenous investment plan formation and information acquisition to jointly analyze the value of investment flexibility and information acquisition to mitigate losses from uncertainty. Our calibrated model, which replicates empirical moments that are informative of investment flexibility and information acquisition, reveals that investment flexibility reduces aggregate productivity losses due to uncertainty by about 41% after accounting for endogenous information acquisition. This translates to about a 1.13% increase in aggregate corporate value when holding aggregate wages fixed, suggesting that accounting for state contingency in investment decisions is important for quantifying the impact of uncertainty on firm value and aggregate productivity.The starting point of our paper is unique firm-level microdata from the Japanese Ministry of Finance (MoF). These data cover 11 years, and one of their key characteristics is that they contain information on firms' annual internal forecasts of sales and profits, investment plans, and actual investment expenditures. This dataset allows us to compare firms' forecasted and actual sales and profits, as well as their investment plans and actual investment expenditures.From this dataset, we establish three stylized facts. First, we find that firms' internal forecasts for revenue productivity are substantially more accurate than a standard AR(1) forecast (the workhorse assumption of models of investment dynamics). For instance, in the cross-section, forecast errors have a standard deviation of about 49% of the volatility of productivity. This implies that firms resolve some of their uncertainty before making an investment decision, echoing prior findings in the literature that managers, broadly speaking, have better information about their own firm than outside observers.2 Second, the accuracy of firms' forecasts is positively related to firms' past productivity; for instance, in the cross-section, a standard deviation increase in past productivity is associated with a 16% standard deviation decrease in the absolute size of forecast errors. Finally, firms partially adjust their investment spending relative to initially budgeted levels in response to real-time shocks, suggesting some flexibility in investment spending. For instance, after controlling for initial investment plans, we find that a 1% increase in realized productivity is associated with a 2.1% increase in investment rates. Altogether, our data suggest that firms rely to some extent on both investment flexibility and information acquisition to mitigate losses from uncertainty.To quantify the importance of these mechanisms, we develop a general-equilibrium neoclassical investment model with heterogeneous firms and capital budgeting to capture the main facts in the data. In our model, capital budgeting involves time-to-build (hereafter TTB) between an investment planning stage, where firms have limited information on demand or productivity and only make partial commitments to some initial investment purchases, to an intermediate stage, where firms have more information and investment decisions are finalized, and the final stage when capital is installed and becomes productive.We emphasize three dimensions of capital budgeting. First, in the planning stage, firms do not observe current productivity but use information on past productivity and new information acquiredвҖ”at some costвҖ”during the initial periods of the current year to forecast current productivity. Second, during an intermediate stage, firms observe actual current productivity and can choose to deviate from their initial investment plans, again at some cost. These two dimensions imply that in the planning stage, firms have an incentive to acquire better information to formulate more precise forecasts of the return on investment, but these incentives depend on the cost of deviation. Third, we allow the costs of information acquisition to be a function of firm productivity, capturing the idea that different types of firms might have access to different technologies for information acquisition. These three dimensions are key to matching the stylized facts above.Based on these primitives, our model further derives a novel theoretical result: The marginal benefit of an additional вҖңunitвҖқ of information for a firm always increases in its productivity, meaning that higher productivity firms have intrinsically greater incentives for acquiring information. Our result implies that information and productivity are complements in production at both the firm and aggregate levels. This is analogous to the predictions of a standard model of physical factor inputs, leading us to interpret information as an intangible factor of production. Moreover, this endogenously gives rise to a distribution of information, which we denote вҖңinformation allocationвҖқ. Given the different incentives to acquire information, our results imply that given a fixed aggregate quantity of resources for information acquisition, the impact of uncertainty on firm values and aggregate productivity depends on both the degree of investment flexibility and the distribution of resources for information acquisition. Because both dimensions of uncertainty are new to the literature, we will evaluate the quantitative relevance of these two mechanisms.Our calibrated baseline model targets empirical moments that are informative of both channels and suggests that aggregate total factor productivity (TFP) loss due to uncertainty is around 0.20%. To estimate the value of investment flexibility, we construct a counterfactual calibrated model replicating the information allocation across firms, but which imposes complete inflexibility in investment. The counterfactual predicts TFP losses of around 0.34%, suggesting that investment flexibility alone reduces the aggregate losses due to uncertainty by about 41%. Along the same lines, holding wages fixed, we find that investment flexibility alone improves aggregate corporate values by around 1.13%. Moreover, while the efficiency gains accrue to all firms, we find that lower-productivity firms are the primary beneficiaries, implying that lower-productivity firms rely substantially more on investment flexibility to mitigate uncertainty.Finally, we quantify how the distribution of resources for information acquisition affects the impact of uncertainty on firms and aggregate productivity. Motivated by the standard assumption that firms face homogeneous uncertainty, our counterfactual imposes a degenerate distribution of information, holding fixed both the level of resources spent on information acquisition and the degree of investment flexibility. The counterfactual predicts TFP losses of around 0.24%вҖ“about 20% higher than our baseline. Along the same lines, holding prices fixed, we find that the information allocation channel improves aggregate corporate values by around 0.32%. Therefore, our model reveals that reallocating the resources for information acquisition to higher-productivity firms, as observed in the data, reduces the aggregate impact of uncertainty. However, unlike investment flexibility, the efficiency gains accrue only to the minority of firms with very high productivity.After discussing the related literature below, the rest of our paper proceeds as follows: Section 2 describes our data and presents stylized evidence that motivates our model; Section 3 describes our model and presents our main theoretical results; Section 4 presents our calibration and results; Section 5 discusses the quantitative model's results; and finally, Section 6 concludes.Related literatureOur results connect to several strands of the macroeconomics and finance literature. First, our paper builds on the large literature in macroeconomics emphasizing the role of time-to-build in investment as a source of business cycle fluctuations and firm risk.3 Beginning with Kydland and Prescott (1982), the canonical assumption of most macroeconomic models of investment under uncertainty is an exogenously fixed period of TTB, following early evidence that TTB is largely technological and insensitive to business cycle shocks (Mayer (1960); Hall (1977); Montgomery (1995)). For instance, in research closely related to our paper, David et al. (2016) assume a TTB of three years when studying the role of information frictions for capital misallocation.In contrast, our paper refines the microfoundation for TTB, postulating that it partly arises from a lag time between when investment plans are made and when investment expenses are accrued. While similar to early research on вҖңtime-to-planвҖқ (Christiano and Todd (1996)), part of our contribution is to present new empirical evidence regarding this dimension of TTB. Moreover, our microfoundation connects to and extends classic work on corporate finance regarding capital budgeting practices, showing that shocks to productivity can lead to inefficient investment because it is difficult to adjust actual investment in real time relative to budgeted capital expenses (e.g., Harris and Raviv 1996; Malenko 2019; Graham 2022). In this sense, our paper is similar to research that studies the degree to which TTB is variable, and its impact on investment decisions (e.g., Bar-Ilan and Strange (1996); Kalouptsidi (2014); Oh and Yoon (2020)). The existing literature has primarily studied variability in the lead time of investment. In contrast, we study a different aspect of TTB вҖ“ that even for a fixed lead time, firms do not bear the full risk of their investment because they can vary investment sizes in a state-contingent fashion. Moreover, we show that the aggregate losses due to information frictions can be overstated if one does not account for this form of risk mitigation.Second, our paper connects to the large literature on informational rigidity and rational inattention (e.g., Sims 2003; Reis 2006; Coibion and Gorodnichenko 2015; see, in particular, the handbook chapter by Baley and Veldkamp 2021). While largely focused on business cycles and inflation dynamics (e.g., Van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp 2009; MaДҮkowiak and Wiederholt 2015; Baley and Blanco 2019; Ilut and Saijo 2020), a small but growing body of related research now studies the role of information frictions as a source of capital misallocation (e.g., David et al. (2016); Tanaka et al. (2020); Gondhi (2023)). Unlike earlier research, a key contribution of our paper is to show theoretically how information acts like an intangible asset and to demonstrate the quantitative relevance of information allocation across firms as a source of aggregate efficiency gains. In this context, our research is similar to Veldkamp (2023) and Farboodi and Veldkamp (2022) in emphasizing information as a firm asset. However, these studies do not focus on the distributional consequences of information allocation, nor do they account for investment flexibility as a substitute for information.Our theoretical results also help inform recent empirical findings on firm expectations formation. Recent findings suggest that higher productivity firms tend to make better forecasts over microeconomic and macroeconomic variables. For instance, Bloom et al. (2021) find that high-productivity firms make better forecasts of future firm revenue, and Tanaka et al. (2020) find similar results for macroeconomic aggregates. Unlike these papers, our model endogenizes information acquisition, explaining empirically observed patterns of managerial forecasting based on rational agents with costly learning. Moreover, our model allows us to relate firm characteristics to the cross-sectional distribution of forecast errors. We argue that this heterogeneity is important for the overall aggregate effects of managerial beliefs. Our framework also allows for several interesting extensions examining the interplay between how firms choose to acquire information and other variables, such as corporate financial policy. In this sense, our approach and inferences are similar to Farboodi et al. (2022), who study the incentives for investors to acquire information and present a comprehensive framework to measure the private value of financial information.Third, our findings relate to the voluminous research studying the sources of capital misallocation following the seminal work of Restuccia and Rogerson (2008) and Hsieh and Klenow (2009). Our paper most directly relates to the literature emphasizing the role of uncertainty, TTB, and capital adjustment frictions as sources of capital misallocation (Asker et al. 2014; David et al. 2016; David and Venkateswaran 2019). However, we additionally emphasize the role of information allocation as a determinant of capital misallocation. Our emphasis on studying the underlying intangible factors backing the distribution of tangible factors of production mirrors recent research in the literature (e.g., Bhandari and McGrattan 2020; Whited and Zhao 2021).Finally, we present new evidence using a unique novel dataset of firm expectations. The setting is also useful because it should provide researchers with a long panel of capital budgets, sales, and profit forecasts, which could be useful broadly in studying corporate investment behavior. Prior studies with investment plans use managerial forecasts made by a small and selected subset of public firms or firm panels with qualitative forecasts regarding sales or investment (e.g., Bachmann et al. 2013; Bachmann and Elstner 2015; Gennaioli et al. 2015; Bachmann et al. 2017; Barrero 2022; Bloom et al. 2021). Other studies involve experiments designed to elicit the expectations formation process of private individuals but do not study capital investment behavior and firm profitability (e.g., Coibion and Gorodnichenko 2015; Coibion et al. 2020; Fuster et al. 2022).

Supporting and motivating evidence: In this section, we first introduce our data source and measurement strategy, and then show stylized facts motivating our study of investment flexibility and information acquisition as strategies that firms adopt during the capital budgeting process to mitigate the negative effects of uncertainty.2.1. Data source, sample selection, and summary statisticsOur sample merges two distinct datasets from the Japanese Ministry of Finance (MoF): the Business Outlook Survey (BOS) and the Annual and Quarterly Financial Statements Statistics of Corporations by Industry. The first dataset contains firm-level forecasts for sales, profits, and investment spending plans and their realizations. Specifically, firms are surveyed around six weeks into the first fiscal quarter about their forecasted sales, profits, and investment spending plans for the full fiscal year. At the end of the year, they are surveyed again regarding their actual sales, profits, and investment spending. We focus on planned and realized plants, property, and equipment (PP&E) as our definition of вҖңinvestmentвҖқ. The second dataset provides year-end (quarter-end) financial statistics such as total assets, cash holdings, debt, total employment, and cost of labor for the fiscal period. Although the two surveys are distinct, they follow the same sampling procedure, target the same firms, and use the same anonymized firm identifiers.Responding to both surveys is mandatory per the Japanese Statistics Act, and the response rates for large firms over our sample period are between around 87% and 89%.4 This similarity in survey procedures and high response rates permit a high-quality merge of the data that creates a nationally representative panel of both large public and large private firms in Japan with information on their expectations and financial statements accounting for over 60% of total employment. Appendix A.1 presents more details of these two data sources and our data processing procedures.Our final sample for analysis spans fiscal years 2005 to 2016 and includes only firms from the manufacturing industry based on the Japanese Standard Industrial Classification (J-SIC) codes. We exclude firm-year observations with zero sales or missing information on labor costs or PP&E. The manufacturing firms in our dataset account for roughly 7 million employees per year, which is about 10% of the Japanese workforce.Table 1 reports the general characteristics of our sample, including the mean, standard deviation, skewness, and the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of key balance sheet variables. The average total assets of a firm in our sample is 87,223.8 million yen (approx. US$615 million) with a median of 20,554 million yen (approx. US$145.5 million), and the average capital stock of 46,816.1 million yen (approx. US$330.2 million) with a median of 8,579 billion yen (approx. US$60.5 million). Measurement strategy for empirical analysis2.2.1. Investment plans and investment flexibilityA key feature of the BOS is that we observe planned investment spending as of the start of the fiscal year as well as actual investment spending over the course of the fiscal year. In the context of our focus on capital budgeting, we interpret вҖңplanned investment spendingвҖқ as pre-committed capital purchases. For instance, in our previous example, Emirates Airlines anticipates capital expenditures associated with purchasing Airbus A380 planes and sets aside that money at the beginning of the year, pre-committing. In turn, actual investment spending reflects the actual number of A380s Emirates Airlines paid for over the course of the year after re-negotiating their contract in response to Covid-19.Our main focus in this study is the degree to which investment spending deviates from investment plans. We define вҖңinvestment plan deviationвҖқ as the difference between realized investment at the end of the fiscal year and planned investment as of the beginning of the fiscal year, deflated by initial capital stock. Deviations may be zero if investment is entirely inflexible or firms perfectly forecast their need for investment, so realized expenditures never deviate from plans even if investment was perfectly flexible. As such, we next show how firm uncertainty over (revenue) productivity forecasts interacts with firms' demand for investment.A caveat for our empirical analysis is that the investment values in the survey data only reflect capital expenses. This means that firms that plan to and/or actually disinvest will report planned investment and/or actual investment of zero. This censoring biases our measure of investment plan deviations toward zero. We discuss this bias in more detail in Appendix A.2.1 and argue that based on simulations, we do not believe that this bias meaningfully affects our empirical results.2.2.2. Productivity and firm uncertaintyInvestment plans are made at the beginning of the fiscal year in the BOS, so our analysis on firm uncertainty likewise focuses on uncertainty over firm outcomes within the fiscal year. We study uncertainty over own-firm productivity, since the typical assumption is that productivity is the main exogenous variable driving investment demand. Forecast errorsвҖ”the difference between realized and forecasted productivityвҖ”serve as a measure of firm uncertainty. Therefore, two key objects that we need to measure are the firm's forecast of productivity as of the beginning of the fiscal year and realized productivity as of the end of the fiscal year based on realized sales.For realized productivity, we follow the literature (e.g., Hsieh and Klenow (2009)) by assuming that firms operate Cobb-Douglas вҖңvalue-added production functionsвҖқ, using capital (k) and labor (l) as inputs with capital intensity Оұ, such that output y is given by y=zЛңkОұl1вҲ’Оұ and where zЛң is physical productivity. In addition, they face isoelastic demand curves with elasticity О·. Given values for Оұ and О·, we can back out physical productivity using the identity(1)zЛң=(py)О·О·вҲ’1kОұl1вҲ’Оұ, where py is the firm's total value-added for the fiscal year. For measurement purposes, k is the firm's physical capital stock measured at the end of the last fiscal year, and l is the number of full-time equivalent labor hired as of the end of the current fiscal year.5 In this framework, we cannot distinguish between вҖңrevenueвҖқ productivity and вҖңphysicalвҖқ productivity (Syverson (2011)), since we only observe income and balance sheet items. Therefore, we simply term zЛң generically as вҖңproductivityвҖқ henceforth, and study uncertainty around zЛң, which forms a single sufficient summary statistic capturing overall uncertainty over demand and output. We use the вҖңtildeвҖқ variable for productivity (zЛң) to denote that this is productivity measured as a residual of the revenue function, which itself is a combination of both a transitory and permanent component of firm productivity. This distinguishes it against z without the вҖңtildeвҖқ, which we use to denote only the transitory component of productivity in Section 3 onwards.We are interested in quantifying the firm's uncertainty around zЛң while investment plans are made at the start of the fiscal year. To that end, we make two standard assumptions. First, we follow convention and assume that logвҒЎzЛң follows a Gaussian AR(1) process with volatility ПғПө. Second, we assume that firms have rational expectations and thus perfect understanding of the data generating process for zЛң. Therefore, uncertainty arises because firms do not have information over the innovation to zЛң, which we denote by Пө. Consequently, the standard deviation of forecast errors for Пө, relative to ПғПө, gives us a measure of a firm's actual uncertainty while making investment plans.Because we do not directly observe forecasted zЛң, only forecasted sales, we compute an approximate forecast error using the formula(2)ПөFEЛҶвүЎlogвҒЎ(py)О·О·вҲ’1kОұl1вҲ’ОұвҲ’logвҒЎ(pye)О·О·вҲ’1kОұl1вҲ’Оұ, where pye is the firm's forecasted value-added for the year (which we compute from forecasted sales), and where for notation, we use the вҖңhatвҖқ variable to denote that this is only an approximate of the true forecast error (which we denote by ПөFE in the rest of the paper). This formula has a natural interpretation of (pye)О·О·вҲ’1kОұl1вҲ’Оұ as the firm's expected productivity.It is important to point out that our formula contains measurement error due to Jensen's inequality. Importantly, this implies that the standard deviation of ПөFEЛҶ is overstated by (i) a factor 11вҲ’(1вҲ’Оұ)О·вҲ’1О·, and (ii) by variations in V across firms. (2022)). Unfortunately, the survey design of the BOS implies that we only observe a single forecast error per firm (and year). Therefore, we instead bin firms into centiles according to their beginning-of-period measured productivity (which we denote by zЛңвҲ’1), and then compute the standard deviation of forecast errors within each bin, which (with abuse of notation) we denote by sd(ПөFEЛҶ|zЛңвҲ’1).Fig. 1b shows a histogram of sd(ПөFEЛҶ|zЛңвҲ’1) normalized by the volatility of zЛң computed within each centile of zвҲ’1. The summary statistics for this ratio are shown in the second row of Table 2. Similar to before, we also pre-multiply ПөFEЛҶ by 1вҲ’(1вҲ’Оұ)О·вҲ’1О·. Table 2 shows that the average ratio is around 51%, with a standard deviation of around 8.5%.For the vast majority of firms, the standard deviation of forecast errors is smaller than its corresponding volatility. For instance, at 3 standard deviations above the mean, the ratio is around 76.5%, implying that most firms resolve some degree of uncertainty over productivity in their forecasting. However, they continue to face non-trivial amounts of remaining uncertainty. For instance, at 3 standard deviations below the mean, the ratio is around 25.5%. While much of the extant research, such as the literature on rational inattention, has focused on studying the ability of firms to partially resolve uncertainty, there is little prior research to help explain the source of heterogeneity in information acquisition that we document. Likewise, there is sparse prior research quantifying the extent of investment flexibility and its implications for firm values and aggregate efficiency. As such, we next turn to describing our model which we build to quantify these mechanisms.
article.3. Model: Our model is a standard neoclassical investment model that incorporates capital budgeting parsimoniously by incorporating three components: (i) information acquisition, where firms can invest in better information to improve the accuracy of their investment plans; (ii) investment plan formation, where firms lay out their investment plans for the year; and (iii) capital expensing, where firms decide how much capital to actually purchase, subject to costly deviation from initial plans. After presenting the model, we characterize the value of information at the firm and aggregate level in such an economic environment to guide our counterfactual analyses later.3.1. Economic environmentWe use a standard setup for agents, households, and final goods producers. We consider a discrete time infinite horizon model with infinitely lived agents. The economy is populated by a representative household and a representative final goods producing firm, and heterogeneous intermediate goods producing firms. Each model period is 1 year, corresponding to our sample frequency. Although the household and final goods firms play a limited role in our analysis, we present their setup as they are necessary for studying the impact of information allocation on aggregate productivity. The representative household discounts time at rate ОІ, inelastically supplies a fixed quantity of labor N=1, and has preferences over consumption of a final aggregate consumption good. The representative final goods firm produces the final aggregate good given by Yt=(вҲ«01yi,tО·вҲ’1О·di)О·О·вҲ’1, where i is a specific intermediate good and О· is the elasticity of substitution across goods. The usual cost minimization problem for the final goods firm yields the standard demand schedule for each good i as pi,t=yi,tвҲ’1О·PtYt1О·, where PtвүЎ(вҲ«01pi,t1вҲ’О·di)11вҲ’О· is the constant elasticity of substitution price index, giving us PtYt=вҲ«01pi,tyi,tdi. For the rest of the paper, we will set the final consumption good as the numeraire and normalize P to 1.Each intermediate goods firm i produces a differentiated good based on the production function yi,t=Ојizi,tki,tОұli,t1вҲ’Оұ, where Ојi is a firm-specific long-run productivity, zi,t is idiosyncratic stochastic productivity, ki,t is the beginning-of-period capital stock, li,t is labor hired by the firm, and ОұвҲҲ(0,1) is the capital share of the firm. For the permanent component of productivity Ојi, we assume that Ојi is drawn from a log-normal distribution with mean 0 and variance ПғОј2. For the persistent component of productivity zi,t, we assume it follows the AR(1) process logвҒЎzi,t=ПҒlogвҒЎzi,tвҲ’1+Пөit, where Пөi,tвҲјN(0,ПғПө2). In addition, capital depreciates at a geometric rate Оҙ. Finally, each unit of labor costs a wage w, and we assume that li,t is chosen after zi,t has been observed. As such, firms choose labor to maximize static profits which are determined by the residual demand function above. Under these assumptions, without loss of generality, we can directly rewrite the firm's gross profit function net of labor cost as(7)ПҖ=A(wt,Yt)ОјiОҳzzi,tОҳzki,tОҳk, where ОҳzвүЎО·вҲ’1О·1вҲ’(1вҲ’Оұ)О·вҲ’1О·, ОҳkвүЎОұО·вҲ’1О·1вҲ’(1вҲ’Оұ)О·вҲ’1О·, and A(w,Y)>0 is a function of the endogenous aggregate wage and output.Besides labor, managers also decide on capital inputs, which are quasi-fixed and determined in the previous period. The role of the manager is to make a sequence of investment decisions to maximize the firm's net present value, which the manager discounts at constant rate 11+r.12 Below, we conceptualize the capital budgeting and investment decision of the manager and discuss how capital budgeting affects the value of the firm.3.2. Capital budgeting and investmentFig. 3 illustrates how the investment problem is broken down into 3 sub-periods to reflect capital budgeting (sub-period I and II) and expensing (sub-period III). First, a manager begins a fiscal year with full information on last period productivity zi,tвҲ’1 but no information about the realization of TFP innovation Пөi,t (and thus, no information about zi,t). Second, the manager must make a capital budget for the year; but before laying out the budget, she has the option to purchase better information about the year's prospects Пөi,t (e.g., by doing costly market research). Finally, after setting a budget, the manager observes the current year's productivity and decides how much labor to hire and how much actual capital to spend on investment. Importantly, any deviation of investment from the capital budget is costly, providing an incentive to make better investment plans at the beginning of the fiscal year. We now discuss each sub-period in more detail below.Download: Download high-res image (441KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. Model timing. This figure illustrates the three sub-periods of our model.Sub-period I: information acquisitionвҖғ The manager begins with full information on last period's productivity zi,tвҲ’1, but does not observe Пөi,t. However, she is able to improve on her information by acquiring a signal with precision 1/Пғi,t2. For some choice of Пғi,t, the manager will receive a signal si,t about current productivity, given by si,t=ui,t+Пөi,t, where ui,tвҲјN(0,Пғi,t2). The manager learns by Bayesian updating: after observing the signal, the manager's belief over the posterior distribution of zi,t is given by logвҒЎzi,tвҲјN(logвҒЎzЛҶi,t,Vi,t), where logвҒЎzЛҶi,tвүЎПҒlogвҒЎzi,tвҲ’1+ПғПө2Пғi,t2+ПғПө2si,t. zЛҶi,t is the expected current period productivity and Vi,t is the posterior variance of productivity (equivalently, Vi,tвҲ’1 is the signal precision). Given our information structure, we can define a TFP shock as follows: Definition 1(TFP shock) A TFP shock (forecast error) is defined as ПөFEвүЎlogвҒЎzi,tвҲ’logвҒЎzЛҶi,t, that is, the log-difference between realized and forecasted TFP. However, information acquisition is costly, and the cost function has the following parametric form:(8)C(Пғi,t,zi,tвҲ’1,Ојi)=ОҫОјiО¶Ојzi,tвҲ’1О¶zПғi,tвҲ’2, where О¶z and О¶Ој control the degree to which information acquisition costs depend on initial productivity zi,tвҲ’1 and long-run productivity Ојi respectively. This reduced-form specification implies that the private marginal cost of improving signal precision is ОҫОјiО¶Ојzi,tвҲ’1О¶z, and allows us to nest a variety of potential learning costs. For instance, one could interpret them as only arising from dynamic- and firm- specific distortions in the spirit of David and Venkateswaran (2019); in this case, the fundamental marginal cost is constant (Оҫ). Alternatively, they could reflect that the learning technology is fundamentally associated with both components of productivity.13Sub-period II: investment plan formationвҖғ After observing the signal and forming an updated belief over the distribution of Пөi,t, the manager makes an investment plan ki,t+1p. We assume as a model primitive that these investment plans have to be made prior to making an actual investment in order to achieve next-period capital stock ki,t+1. In turn, managers have an incentive to make accurate plans because it is costly to deviate from the plan. Specifically, given some plans and actual investment, the manager faces the following cost function П•(ki,t+1p,ki,t+1) for any deviation from the plan:(9)П•(ki,t+1p,ki,t+1)=ПҮ2(ki,t+1ki,t+1pвҲ’1)2ki,t+1p, where ПҮвүҘ0 captures the flexibility of the manager to deviate from the initial planned investment.Although the parametrization of the cost function is analogous to a traditional convex physical adjustment cost function, it is a penalty for investment plan deviations rather than deviations from the firm's current capital stock.14 We show later that our qualitative insights regarding information acquisition do not hinge on this functional form assumption, but this assumption permits us to more closely match the data in our quantitative exercise.Sub-period III: investmentвҖғ After making an investment plan, the manager perfectly observes current period productivity zi,t. Knowing that zi,t follows an AR(1) process, the manager uses her updated information to decide how much capital to invest for use in the next period, subject to the aforementioned adjustment cost П•(ki,t+1p,ki,t+1) in Equation (9). Given our functional form assumption, it is convenient to define an investment plan deviation as follows: Definition 2(Investment Plan Deviation) An investment plan deviation is defined as xi,tвүЎlogвҒЎki,t+1вҲ’logвҒЎki,t+1p, that is, the log-difference between realized and planned next-period capital. Before moving on to the next subsection, we clarify the interpretation of kp and the cost of deviating from kp. While we have termed kp as an вҖңinvestment planвҖқ, in practice, it captures a multitude of real-world decisions associated with capital budgeting and investment. For instance, it might reflect pre-committed capital purchases, as in our example in the introduction; in turn, the cost of deviation reflects the penalty to cancel or restructure these purchases. Alternatively, it can also be micro-founded by operational inefficiencies such as those modeled by Harris and Raviv (1996) and Malenko (2019); in turn, the cost of deviation reflects the renegotiation costs involved between a project manager and upper management.In the broader context of the macroeconomics literature, our assumption on investment timing relates to the idea of time-to-build and time-to-plan (e.g., Kydland and Prescott (1982); Christiano and Todd (1996)). Our investment problem implies a total time-to-build of two years, which maps directly to our empirical analysis, and is consistent with empirical research (e.g., Mayer (1960); Montgomery (1995); Kalouptsidi (2014)). In turn, ПҮ captures the ability of the firm to structure investment decisions in a state-contingent fashion in the first year, and thus quantifies the degree to which time-to-build or time-to-plan is in fact constraining to the firm. This is unique to our model, contrasting with standard assumptions where completely inflexible investment plans (i.e., ПҮвҶ’вҲһ) are model primitives, and a key focus of our later quantitative analysis.3.3. Bellman equationsWe can now define the problem recursively. For a firm with a generic Ој, let JОј(z,k,kp) denote the value function of the manager after all shocks have been realized (i.e., at sub-period III), WОј(zвҲ’1,s,k,Пғ) the value function of the manager after a signal has been observed (i.e., sub-period II), and finally VОј(zвҲ’1,k) the value function of the manager at the beginning of the period (i.e., sub-period I). The manager knows the productivity from the previous period zвҲ’1.3.4. Equilibrium definitionA stationary competitive equilibrium is defined by a measure of firms Оӣ; a set of policy functions {Пғ(Ој,zвҲ’1,k;w,Y),kp(Ој,zвҲ’1,k,s;w,Y),kвҖІ(Ој,zвҲ’1,k,s,z;w,Y)}; a set of value functions {VОј,WОј,JОј}; intermediate good prices {pi,t}iвҲҲОӣ; wage w; and a Markov transition function О“ induced by the policy function kвҖІ and exogenous productivity zвҖІ such that (1) the distribution of firms is invariant (Оӣ=О“(Оӣ)), (2) the labor market clears, and (3) the intermediate goods market clears.3.5. Characterizing the value and costs of informationIn our model, information frictions depress firm value and lead to capital misallocation because investment is subject to a time-to-build friction. As such, the impact of information frictions must be decreasing in investment flexibility. In the latter sections, our quantitative model will allow us to quantify the value of investment flexibility accounting for such information frictions.However, it is unclear how the information frictions affect the value of information on net, holding fixed investment flexibility. Specifically, we are interested in using our model to rationalize our empirical observation that there is a negative correlation between productivity and uncertainty in the data, as well as to why the correlation of productivity and uncertainty is stronger within firm. As such, for the rest of this subsection, we will focus on presenting a characterization of the value of information for firms and the aggregate economy with the simplifying assumption that ПҮ=вҲһ. We then build on these insights to examine their quantitative implications in Section 5. Appendix C.3 shows that these same insights apply to our more general model with investment flexibility.3.5.1. What drives heterogeneity in information acquisition?To begin, we first state the following formal proposition, which will be useful for the discussion. Importantly, we show that under standard assumptions on the profit function, heterogeneity in uncertainty already arises independently of the technology for information acquisition. Appendix C contains all relevant formal proofs for the propositions below. Appendix C.3 shows that the following results also apply to our more general model. Proposition 1(Marginal Benefit of Learning) Suppose ПҮ=вҲһ. That is, information and productivity are complements at the firm-level. Proposition 1 summarizes two aspects of information. First, it points out that information is desirable, and all firms prefer to face lower uncertainty since it increases firm value. For instance, just as Оҳk captures the overall decreasing returns to capital in our model (Equation (7)), the term вҲӮFПҖ(VвҲ’1)вҲӮVвҲ’1 captures the degree of decreasing returns to information. Likewise, just as the marginal product of capital increases in productivity, so does the marginal product of information.Our main result here is that while information is not an explicit input into production, we can re-express all physical factor inputs as functions of information choices because the investment decision is tied back to the information set of the firm during the capital budgeting process. Thus, one can interpret information as an intangible asset and price the value of a firm in terms of its sequence of information choices.Moreover, this reveals that even if firms had access to the same technology for information acquisition, we would already see an endogenous distribution of uncertainty because firms invest differentially into information acquisition based on their productivity. Therefore, this allows us to interpret heterogeneity in uncertainty as a form of factor allocation, which we term вҖңinformation allocationвҖқ.It is important to point out that the impact of Ој and zвҲ’1 on the value of information derives from fundamentally different mechanisms. In the case of Ој, this has the standard interpretation that permanently higher productivity firms have higher incentives to invest in more assets, and are thus larger firms in the longer run.In contrast, the correlation of the marginal benefit with zвҲ’1 captures how firm incentives fluctuate with idiosyncratic shocks to firm productivity. In turn, this reflects the incentives of a firm to dynamically reallocate resources dedicated to information acquisition, investing more in good times and less in bad times. Noticeably, unlike Ој, the dependence on zвҲ’1 is a function of ПҒ, the persistence of productivity. This reflects the fact that zвҲ’1 affects information acquisition decisions only to the extent that it affects the ex-dividend net present value of the firm. This mechanism is analogous to a standard neoclassical model of investment under uncertainty, where the marginal propensity to invest is a decreasing function of ПҒ.Given the above discussion, Equation (13) implies that if firms had homogeneous technologies for information acquisition, it must be that productivity is more strongly associated with the permanent, rather than transitory, component of productivity. While this theoretical result runs against our empirical findings at first blush, our theoretical analysis so far has only focused on the benefit side of the equation, but actual investment decisions trade off both marginal benefits and costs. Because standard models impose homogeneous uncertainty, it must be that О¶Ој=Оҳz1вҲ’Оҳk and О¶z=ПҒ2Оҳz1вҲ’Оҳk; that is, marginal cost and benefit exactly balance out. Viewed through the lens of our model, this implies a unique assumption on the allocation of information across firms. We now turn to the aggregate implications of such an assumption.3.5.2. Information allocation and aggregate productivityWe begin by first presenting an aggregation result relating arbitrary allocations of information to aggregate total factor productivity (TFP), using the simplified version of our model with ПҮ=вҲһ. We define TFPвүЎ(вҲ«yО·вҲ’1О·dОӣ)О·О·вҲ’1(вҲ«kdОӣ)Оұ(вҲ«ldОӣ)1вҲ’Оұ, where Оӣ is the distribution of firms, and Proposition 2 states the result. Proposition 2For any given marginal distributions of initial period productivity zвҲ’1, long-run productivity Ој, and information choice V, aggregate TFP is decreasing in the correlation between productivity and V. The result is analogous to a model where the distribution of physical factor inputs is arbitrarily imposed, and the total stock of factors is fixed such that TFP gains arise solely from factor reallocation. In the context of physical factors, because productivity and factors of production are explicitly modeled as complements, it is obvious that TFP would increase if relatively more factors are reallocated towards higher productivity firms.One of our contributions is to show that a similar result holds for productivity and informationвҖ”an implicit factor of productionвҖ”where such complementarity is neither explicit nor obvious at face value. Importantly, based on our empirical findings, this implies that conventional models of investment under uncertainty must overstate the degree of misallocation due to uncertainty since the standard assumption is that productivity and uncertainty are uncorrelated. Quantifying the importance of this channel will be a key part of our quantitative exercise.
article.4. Model calibration: In this section, we discuss our model calibration and results. Our model parameter values are determined using the data discussed in Section 2.1. We then use our calibrated model to conduct counterfactual analyses in the next section.4.1. Calibration strategyWe follow standard approaches in the macroeconomics literature in calibrating our model. First, we fix a subset of parameters based on values from existing literature: r=0.02, consistent with an annual real interest rate of 2%; Оҙ=0.06 consistent with an annual depreciation rate of 6%; and set N=1 and P=1 as a normalization. Next, we set another subset of parameters (Оұ,О·,ПғПө,ПҒ,ПғОј) based on direct estimation from the data. Finally, we set the remaining four key parameters (Оҫ,ПҮ,О¶z,О¶Ој) in general equilibrium by matching key moments that identify these parameters.4.1.1. Externally estimated parametersIn order to obtain Оұ and О·, we need values of both the labor and capital revenue elasticities (which evaluate to (1вҲ’Оұ)О·вҲ’1О· and ОұО·вҲ’1О· respectively in our model). This result presents an interesting contrast to recent research that hypothesize that high-productivity firms have an absolute advantage in forecasting (i.e., lower cost for higher productivity firms) as a reason for why high-productivity firms make more precise forecasts of profitability and related fundamentals (e.g., Tanaka et al. Quantitative model implications: In this section, we use our calibrated model to separately evaluate the impact of uncertainty on firm values and aggregate productivity when investment decisions are partially flexible, and when the distribution of resources towards information acquisition is allocated towards higher productivity firms, as in the data. For each exercise, we begin by formalizing the appropriate counterfactual for comparison, then analyze the impact of these channels on private firm value and aggregate productivity in both partial and general equilibrium.5.1. The value of investment flexibility5.1.1. Defining counterfactualsTo quantify the value of investment flexibility, we set ПҮ=вҲһ and impose two constraints: Across both baseline and counterfactual, (i) the total вҖңquantity of informationвҖқ in the economy is constant and (ii) the allocation of information is constant. By imposing these constraints, we ensure that we are purely isolating the impact of investment flexibility rather than conflating its impact with changes in the total level of resources in aggregate on acquiring information or the distribution of those resources.To implement the first constraint, we define the aggregate quantity of information as вҲ«VвҲ’1(Ој,zвҲ’1)dОӣ. For some generic information acquisition policy function VвҲ’1 and distribution of firms Оӣ, total information is quantified as the average precision of information across all firms. Therefore, the aggregate resource constraint for information can be defined as вҲ«VnвҲ’1dОӣnвүӨвҲ«VoвҲ’1dОӣo, where the subscripts o and n refer to the baseline (original) and counterfactual (new) models, and the constraint implies that we cannot create more information than already exists.To implement the second constraint, we impose the condition that the correlation of productivity with a firm's uncertainty is identical to the baseline model. As a result, because the only source of heterogeneity in uncertainty derives from heterogeneity in productivity, this implies that the allocation of information is identical in both economies. Imposing both constraints therefore amounts to finding a new set of Оҫ, О¶Ој, and О¶z until we find a set of policy functions VnвҲ’1 such that the above constraints hold with equality.195.1.2. In other words, we compute the ex-dividend value of a firm using our Bellman equations (Equations (10) to (12)), subtracting the dollar cost of capital but not information acquisition or investment plan adjustment costs, since we do not consider them as direct costs of operations in our baseline. We net out the dividend flow at period 0 (i.e., ПҖ0вҲ’(r+Оҙ)k0) because it does not depend on the capital budgeting decision of the firm at time 0. Both figures are plotted for the analysis under partial equilibrium.We find that investment flexibility generates gains to firm value across-the-board, which is not surprising since we have also constrained firms from acquiring more information to bypass their restriction on ex post adjustments. Moreover, the value of flexibility decreases in productivity. In other words, the relative gains to firm value from being flexible is lower for a higher productivity firm. This reflects the fact that in our data, uncertainty is decreasing in firm productivity and consequently implies that high-productivity firms rely on reducing ex ante uncertainty to improve firm value rather than adjusting their investment decisions ex post. We also find that the magnitude of ОІОј is almost ten times that of ОІz; in other words, the value of flexibility primarily differs across firms of different types, but varies much less across idiosyncratic states. This result is also reflected in our illustrative plot (Fig. 6b), where one can see visually that while О”О  varies widely with Ој, the variation along zвҲ’1 is much smaller. Therefore, the value of flexibility primarily comes from static differences across firms.Finally, we find that the value of flexibility is more muted in general equilibrium. For instance, average gains to firm value is about one-third that in the partial equilibrium counterfactual (Column 5), while aggregate gains to the value of the corporate sector is around one-tenth that in the partial equilibrium counterfactual (Column 6). The difference between the two analyses arises from net changes in the demand for labor and our assumption that labor supply is inelastic. Due to better capital allocation in the baseline, labor demand is substantially higher (Fig. B.4 in Appendix B.3 provides an illustrative plot), which translates to much higher wages in the baseline due to inelastic labor supply. Consequently, firm revenue and profits are relatively lower when we account for general equilibrium effects. Notably, while demand for firm output is also higher in equilibrium (which in turn increases firm value across-the-board), the rise in labor cost is sharper than that of demand (decreasing firm profits on net).5.1.3. Aggregate value of flexibilityFinally, we quantify the aggregate value of investment flexibility. We build on the discussion above by computing TFP in the model of interest relative to a model where firms face no uncertainty about current productivity. We find that our baseline model predicts about 0.20% TFP loss due to uncertainty (Column 1 of Table 8), whereas our counterfactual model with no investment flexibility predicts TFP losses that are around 0.34% (Column 2 of Table 8). Put differently, this result implies that investment flexibility alone reduces capital misallocation due to uncertainty by about 41% (i.e., 0.20/0.34). In terms of tangible output, these aggregate productivity gains imply that GDP in our baseline model is 0.85% higher in the partial equilibrium comparison, and 0.36% higher in the general equilibrium comparison (Column 2 of Table 8).Table 8. The value of information reallocation5.2.1. Defining counterfactualsThe data (and thus our calibrated model) are consistent with the notion that more productive firms endogenously acquire more information, and thus implies that resources for information acquisition are allocated towards higher productivity firms. This differs from the standard assumption that firms face homogeneous uncertainty where, as a result, the distribution of information is implicitly degenerate. Our goal is to quantify the benefits of such information reallocation, in light of our earlier theoretical result.To conduct this exercise, we impose in the counterfactual that the net benefit of information is homogeneous, following the standard assumption in the extant literature that uncertainty is homogeneous across firms. This amounts to setting О¶Ој=Оҳz1вҲ’Оҳk and О¶z=ПҒ2Оҳz1вҲ’Оҳk (Equation (14)). We then further impose two constraints: across both baseline and counterfactual models, (i) the total quantity of information in the economy is constant (like in the earlier exercise) and, (ii) the average degree of investment flexibility is identical. For the second constraint, we recalibrate ПҮ such that the dispersion of investment plan deviations is identical across both models, since we interpret investment plans deviations as a measure of flexibility.5.2.2. Private gains to information reallocationOur first analysis focuses on the impact of information allocation on firm value. As with the investment flexibility analysis, we use Equation (18) to define firm value, and Equation (19) to quantify the gains from heterogeneous information allocation relative to the counterfactual (i.e., relative to the economy with homogeneous uncertainty).In Panel B of Table 7, we report statistics associated with the distribution of О”О (Ој,zвҲ’1), similar to our analysis earlier; and in Fig. 6b, display illustrative plots of О”О  as a function of Ој for three values of zвҲ’1.21In general, we see that there is a mix of gains and losses across firms, unlike in the case of investment flexibility. In partial equilibrium, we see that around 52.7% of firms report a loss; that is, they prefer an economy where information is homogeneously allocated. The values for ОІz and ОІОј are positive, indicating that the gains are increasing (losses are decreasing) in productivity. This implies that the gains to better information allocation accrue to firms with higher productivity. Moreover, because we imposed an aggregate resource constraint on information, our calibration implies a reallocation of information from low- to high-productivity firms. In other words, the losers of this reallocation are lower productivity firms.Like with investment flexibility, we also find that the magnitude of ОІОј is almost ten times that of ОІz. In other words, the gains to information allocation accrue primarily to firms of permanently higher productivity. In contrast, there are much smaller gains to be made from dynamic reallocation. This follows from the fact that the autocorrelation of productivity is quite small in the data. Hence, the dynamic gains from information acquisition are also small. Importantly, as we discussed in Section 4, our results are a lower bound to the dynamic gains, as we likely underestimate the persistence of productivity.Finally, comparing the partial and general equilibrium results, we again see that gains are much more muted in general equilibrium. In the latter, we see that around 91.8% of firms report a loss. Like before, this difference arises because of a sharp rise in wages due to higher labor demand induced by the better capital allocation. In particular, labor demand by high-productivity firms increases while that by low-productivity firms decreases, since information gets reallocated from low-productivity firms to high-productivity firms in this counterfactual (Fig. B.4 in Appendix B.3). But since aggregate labor demand is disproportionally driven by high-productivity firms, aggregate labor demand increases, driving up equilibrium wages.5.2.3. Aggregate gains to information reallocationWe now turn to quantifying the aggregate value of improved information allocation. Here, our counterfactual model with homogeneous information allocation predicts TFP losses that are around 0.24% (Row 1, Column 3 of Table 8), implying that improved information allocation alone reduces capital misallocation due to uncertainty by about 17% (0.20/0.24). Translating these aggregate productivity gains into tangible output, we find that having an allocation of information that is skewed towards higher productivity firms leads to an increase in GDP of around 0.24% in partial-equilibrium, and a smaller increase of 0.10% in general-equilibrium (Column 3 of Table 8).5.3. Sensitivity analysesWe now briefly describe three sets of sensitivity analyses that we report and discuss in the Appendix.Alternative estimates for Оұ and ОҪвҖғ As discussed in Section 4, we use three complementary methods for estimating the production function parameters (and thus for calculating TFP), and find that our motivating evidence is consistent across all three methods. We conduct an analogous exercise using moments constructed from all three alternatives. Our moments and parameter estimates are listed in Appendix F.3.1 and Appendix F.3.2. We report the results of our quantitative exercise in Appendix F.3.4. Overall, the range of results support a generally robust conclusion that flexibility provides a majority of firms, but particularly small firms, significant protection against uncertainty, while information allocation provides a smaller but sizeable benefit to larger firms.Alternative estimates for ПҒ and ПғПөвҖғ We explore alternative estimates of ПҒ (and ПғПө) to account for the possibility that the ПҒ estimated via OLS is biased downwards. In analyses summarized in Appendix G, we obtain separate estimates using the dynamic panel approaches of Arellano and Bond (1991) and Han and Phillips (2010). As expected, both estimators give rise to higher values of ПҒ. Because the quantitative importance of the information allocation effect is increasing in ПҒ, we find that the information allocation effect is larger when we parametrize our model with these alternative estimates. However, the quantitative importance of investment flexibility is robust across these specifications. In general, we show that our results are similar in that they emphasize both flexibility and information allocation as playing important roles in mitigating the impact of uncertainty for firm values and aggregate productivity.Real costs instead of utility costsвҖғ We explore an alternative assumption where the cost of information acquisition and investment plan adjustments are real costs, instead of utility costs. While these assumptions do not fundamentally affect the partial equilibrium inferences (whether the costs are utility or real is irrelevant in the firm's problem), they can affect firm learning and investment decisions through general equilibrium effects because they compete for resources. In Appendix H, we consider a version where these costs are paid out in labor units, building on our microfoundation in Appendix E. We find largely similar results to our baseline exercise, and importantly, that the parameter estimates are not meaningfully different relative to our baseline.
article.6. Conclusion: In this paper, we develop a theory of capital budgeting as a source of time-to-build and uncertainty, featuring a trade-off between information acquisition for improved capital budgeting against the cost of adjusting investment expenditures ex post to address mistakes made during the capital budgeting process. We theoretically and quantitatively examine the interplay of these two forces and further uncover that information can be thought of as a factor of production like labor and capital: Firms of higher productivity benefit more from better information. We show that holding fixed aggregate resources devoted to information acquisition fixed and reallocating resources for information acquisition towards higher productivity firms can reduce aggregate capital misallocation due to uncertainty and time-to-build. Importantly, we find empirical support for this channel.Although both channels of investment flexibility and information allocation can theoretically mitigate the impact of uncertainty on aggregate productivity and firm value, the data suggest investment flexibility as a more dominant mechanism. These results sharpen our understanding of the costs of uncertainty for firms and emphasize the quantitative importance of the capital budgeting process when studying firm investment decisions. Finally, given the parsimonious model structure and conservative assumptions, our quantitative results are a lower-bound estimate of the importance of investment flexibility as a source of self-insurance against unexpected shocks.
